vulnerability.title: Disable Accepting Router Advertisements on all IPv6 Interfaces by Default
vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_ra kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_ra=0 To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_ra = 0
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Comment out any occurrences of net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_ra from /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files for f in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf; do # skip systemd-sysctl symlink (/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf -> /etc/sysctl.conf) if [[ "$(readlink -f "$f")" == "/etc/sysctl.conf" ]]; then continue; fi matching_list=$(grep -P '^(?!#).*[\s]*net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_ra.*$' $f | uniq ) if ! test -z "$matching_list"; then while IFS= read -r entry; do escaped_entry=$(sed -e 's|/|\\/|g' <<< "$entry") # comment out "net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_ra" matches to preserve user data sed -i --follow-symlinks "s/^${escaped_entry}$/# &/g" $f done <<< "$matching_list" fi done # # S

triage_decision.reason: Disabling IPv6 router advertisements via a sysctl is a standard hardening step that does not affect authentication, authorization, or critical services. It only changes network configuration and is safe to apply without risk of lockout.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: networking affected; IPv6 routing; network configuration

previous_review_verdicts.feedback:
The plan as described does not guarantee that the parameter will be set to 0 after execution. The initial pass comments out any active setting, but the subsequent check for an existing configuration will still detect the commented line and skip creating a new file, leaving the system with no effective setting. This flaw means the vulnerability may persist.

To ensure the vulnerability is fully resolved, the script should either: 1) Explicitly check for an *uncommented* line that sets the parameter to 0 after the comment pass, or 2) Always create/overwrite a file such as /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-ra.conf that contains net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_ra = 0, regardless of whether previous commented lines exist. Adding an explicit runtime apply (sysctl -w ...) is good practice for immediate remediation.
concerns: Potentially leaves the parameter unset after commenting all lines because the new config file is not created when they exist only as commented lines.
The sed command may miss lines with other syntaxes (e.g., tabs or preceding whitespace) if the regular expression is not exhaustive.
The script does not protect the file permission and ownership of the new config file, which could be altered by privileged users.
suggested_improvements:
Modify the check for existing configuration to verify an uncommented line that sets the value to 0; if none, write the new file.
Consider always applying the new file (or forcing overwrite) to guarantee the setting.
Add safeguards to preserve correct file ownership (root:root) and permissions (0644).
Change the sed pattern to handle tabs and spaces robustly: '^\s*net\.ipv6\.conf\.default\.accept_ra\b\s*=.*$'.
Ensure the script runs idempotently by verifying whether sysctl is already set before commenting.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The proposed plan will successfully disable router advertisements by ensuring net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_ra is set to 0 in a persistent sysctl configuration file. The plan appropriately comments out any existing active settings and guarantees that the desired value is applied at boot and runtime. Though not minimal, it addresses the vulnerability without introducing new risks.
concerns: If the priority of the created config file is low (e.g., 99-sysctl.conf can be overridden by later files);
The script may still comment out lines that use different whitespace or tabs if the regular expression is not fully robust;
Current plan does not explicitly set file ownership/permissions after creation in case of permission changes during execution.
suggested_improvements: Use a consistent file name with a high priority (e.g., 90-disable-ra.conf) to avoid being overridden;
Adjust the sed/grep pattern to handle leading whitespace and tabs, e.g., '^\s*net\.ipv6\.conf\.default\.accept_ra\b\s*=.*$';
Explicitly set file ownership to root:root and permissions to 0644 after creation;
Apply the new setting immediately with 'sysctl -p /etc/sysctl.d/90-disable-ra.conf' or 'sysctl -w net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_ra=0' at the end of the script;
Add a check to verify that the runtime value matches the persistent value before concluding.
